questions.stem: A constant net force of $12\ \text{N}$ acts on a $3\ \text{kg}$ block resting on a frictionless surface. What is the block's acceleration?
questions.explanation: Newton's second law gives $a = F/m = 12/3 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $36\ \text{m/s}^2$, which multiplies force by mass instead of dividing, inverting the relationship $F = ma$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A book rests on a table. According to Newton's third law, what is the reaction to the gravitational pull of the Earth on the book?
options: The gravitational pull of the book on the Earth.
The weight of the book pressing on the table.
The normal force of the book on the table.
The normal force of the table on the book.
questions.explanation: A third-law pair acts on two different bodies and is the same type of force. The Earth pulls the book down gravitationally, so the reaction is the book pulling the Earth up gravitationally, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. The trap is the normal force of the table on the book: that force balances the weight in the first law, but it acts on the same body and is a contact force, not the gravitational reaction.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $5\ \text{kg}$ object hangs at rest from a single vertical rope attached to the ceiling. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the tension in the rope?
questions.explanation: The object is in equilibrium, so the net force is zero and the tension balances the weight: $T = mg = 5 \times 10 = 50\ \text{N}$. The trap is $100\ \text{N}$, which doubles the weight, perhaps by adding an imagined reaction force to the actual weight; only one upward force, the tension, acts on the object.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ block rests on a rough horizontal floor. The coefficient of static friction is $0.5$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction is $0.3$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. A horizontal force of $15\ \text{N}$ is applied. What is the friction force on the block?
questions.explanation: First test whether the block moves. The maximum static friction is $\mu_s N = 0.5(40) = 20\ \text{N}$. The applied $15\ \text{N}$ is less than this, so the block stays put and static friction simply matches the applied force at $15\ \text{N}$. The trap is $12\ \text{N}$, which jumps to kinetic friction $\mu_k N = 0.3(40) = 12\ \text{N}$ even though the block never starts sliding.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $10\ \text{kg}$ crate slides across a level floor. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the crate and floor is $0.3$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the kinetic friction force opposing the motion?
questions.explanation: On a level floor the normal force equals the weight, $N = mg = 100\ \text{N}$. The kinetic friction force is $f_k = \mu_k N = 0.3(100) = 30\ \text{N}$. The trap is $3\ \text{N}$, which multiplies the coefficient by the mass alone and omits the factor of $g$ in the weight.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A block is placed on a rough plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.6$. At what angle does the block just begin to slide?
questions.explanation: On the verge of sliding, the component of gravity along the plane, $mg\sin\theta$, equals the maximum static friction $\mu_s mg\cos\theta$. Cancelling $mg$, $\tan\theta = \mu_s = 0.6$, so $\theta = \arctan(0.6) \approx 31^\circ$. This angle of repose depends only on $\mu_s$, not on the mass. The trap is $\arcsin(0.6) \approx 37^\circ$, which sets $\sin\theta = \mu_s$ and ignores the $\cos\theta$ in the normal force.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block slides down a frictionless plane inclined at $30^\circ$ to the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the magnitude of its acceleration?
questions.explanation: On a frictionless incline the only force along the plane is the gravity component $mg\sin\theta$, so the acceleration is $a = g\sin\theta = 10\sin 30^\circ = 10(0.5) = 5\ \text{m/s}^2$, independent of mass. The trap is $8.7\ \text{m/s}^2$, which uses $g\cos 30^\circ$ and confuses the along-plane component with the perpendicular one.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block slides down a plane inclined at $37^\circ$ ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$). The coefficient of kinetic friction is $0.5$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the block's acceleration down the plane?
questions.explanation: Along the plane, gravity pulls with $g\sin\theta = 10(0.6) = 6\ \text{m/s}^2$ worth of acceleration, while kinetic friction opposes it with $\mu_k g\cos\theta = 0.5(10)(0.8) = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The net acceleration is $a = g(\sin\theta - \mu_k\cos\theta) = 6 - 4 = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $6\ \text{m/s}^2$, the frictionless value, which forgets to subtract the friction term.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: An Atwood machine has masses $3\ \text{kg}$ and $5\ \text{kg}$ hanging from a light string over a frictionless pulley. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the acceleration of the masses?
questions.explanation: Treat the two masses as one system driven by the weight difference. The net driving force is $(m_2 - m_1)g = (5 - 3)(10) = 20\ \text{N}$ and the total mass is $m_1 + m_2 = 8\ \text{kg}$, so $a = 20/8 = 2.5\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $1.25\ \text{m/s}^2$, which divides by twice the total mass, as if both masses were counted on each side.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: In an Atwood machine with masses $3\ \text{kg}$ and $5\ \text{kg}$ over a frictionless pulley, the acceleration is $2.5\ \text{m/s}^2$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation: Apply Newton's second law to the lighter mass, which accelerates upward: $T - m_1 g = m_1 a$, so $T = m_1(g + a) = 3(10 + 2.5) = 37.5\ \text{N}$. Checking on the heavier mass, $m_2 g - T = m_2 a$ gives $T = 5(10 - 2.5) = 37.5\ \text{N}$, the same value. The trap is $30\ \text{N}$, which sets the tension equal to the weight of the lighter mass $m_1 g$ and ignores its acceleration.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $3\ \text{kg}$ block on a frictionless horizontal table is connected by a light string over a frictionless pulley at the edge to a $2\ \text{kg}$ block hanging freely. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the acceleration of the system?
questions.explanation: Only the hanging weight drives the system, while both masses share the inertia. The net force is $m_2 g = 2(10) = 20\ \text{N}$ and the total moving mass is $m_1 + m_2 = 5\ \text{kg}$, so $a = 20/5 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $6.7\ \text{m/s}^2$, which divides the driving weight by the hanging mass alone, forgetting that the table block must also be accelerated.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: For the system of the previous setup ($3\ \text{kg}$ on the table, $2\ \text{kg}$ hanging, frictionless, $a = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$, $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$), what is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation: The tension is the only horizontal force on the table block, so $T = m_1 a = 3(4) = 12\ \text{N}$. A cross-check on the hanging block gives $m_2 g - T = m_2 a$, so $T = 2(10 - 4) = 12\ \text{N}$, agreeing. The trap is $20\ \text{N}$, which sets the tension equal to the full hanging weight $m_2 g$ as if nothing were accelerating.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two blocks of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and $4\ \text{kg}$ sit side by side in contact on a frictionless floor. A horizontal force of $12\ \text{N}$ pushes on the $2\ \text{kg}$ block, which in turn pushes the $4\ \text{kg}$ block. What is the contact force between the two blocks?
questions.explanation: The whole system accelerates at $a = F/(m_1 + m_2) = 12/6 = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$. The contact force is the only force on the rear $4\ \text{kg}$ block, so it equals $m_2 a = 4(2) = 8\ \text{N}$. The trap is $12\ \text{N}$, which assumes the full applied force is transmitted to the second block, forgetting that part of it goes into accelerating the first block.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A person of mass $60\ \text{kg}$ stands on a bathroom scale in a lift that accelerates upward at $2\ \text{m/s}^2$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What does the scale read (the normal force on the person)?
questions.explanation: The scale reads the normal force $N$. Newton's second law on the person, taking up as positive, gives $N - mg = ma$, so $N = m(g + a) = 60(10 + 2) = 720\ \text{N}$. The upward acceleration makes the person feel heavier. The trap is $600\ \text{N}$, the ordinary weight $mg$, which would be the reading only if the lift were at rest or moving at constant velocity.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ block rests on a plane inclined at $60^\circ$ to the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the normal force exerted by the plane on the block?
questions.explanation: Perpendicular to the incline the block is in equilibrium, so the normal force balances the perpendicular component of weight: $N = mg\cos\theta = 4(10)\cos 60^\circ = 40(0.5) = 20\ \text{N}$. The trap is $34.6\ \text{N}$, which uses $mg\sin 60^\circ$ and confuses the perpendicular component with the along-plane component.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A car of mass $1000\ \text{kg}$ rounds a flat curve of radius $50\ \text{m}$ at $15\ \text{m/s}$. What is the minimum coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road that prevents skidding? Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$.
questions.explanation: Friction supplies the centripetal force, so at the limit $\mu mg = mv^2/r$, giving $\mu = v^2/(rg) = 225/(50 \times 10) = 225/500 = 0.45$. The mass cancels. The trap is $0.90$, which uses $g = 5\ \text{m/s}^2$ in the denominator (halving $g$ doubles the result), a careless substitution.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $6\ \text{kg}$ mass is pulled along a frictionless floor by a rope that makes an angle of $60^\circ$ above the horizontal with a tension of $20\ \text{N}$. What is the horizontal acceleration of the mass?
questions.explanation: Only the horizontal component of the tension drives the horizontal motion: $T\cos 60^\circ = 20(0.5) = 10\ \text{N}$. Then $a = 10/6 \approx 1.67\ \text{m/s}^2$. The vertical component changes the normal force but, with no friction, does not affect the horizontal acceleration. The trap is $3.33\ \text{m/s}^2$, which uses the full tension $20\ \text{N}$ and ignores that the rope pulls partly upward.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: An object moves at constant velocity in a straight line across a horizontal surface. Which statement about the forces on it must be true?
options: The net force on it is zero.
No forces act on it at all.
There is a net forward force keeping it moving.
The friction force must be zero.
questions.explanation: By Newton's first law, constant velocity means zero acceleration, which requires the net force to be zero. Individual forces such as an applied push and friction may both be present, but they cancel. The trap is the idea that a net forward force is needed to keep the object moving; that belief is the pre-Newtonian view, and in fact a net force would change the velocity rather than maintain it.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A mass hangs from two light strings attached to the ceiling, each making $30^\circ$ with the vertical, so the strings are symmetric. The mass is $2\ \text{kg}$ and $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the tension in each string?
questions.explanation: The vertical components of the two tensions support the weight: $2T\cos 30^\circ = mg$. So $T = mg/(2\cos 30^\circ) = 20/(2 \times 0.866) = 20/1.732 \approx 11.5\ \text{N}$. The horizontal components cancel by symmetry. The trap is $10\ \text{N}$, which simply splits the weight in two as $mg/2$ and forgets that each string is tilted, so only part of its tension acts vertically.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $1\ \text{kg}$ ball is whirled in a vertical circle of radius $1\ \text{m}$ on a string. At the top of the circle the ball moves at $5\ \text{m/s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the tension in the string at the top?
questions.explanation: At the top, both the tension and the weight point downward, toward the centre, and together they supply the centripetal force: $T + mg = mv^2/r$. So $T = mv^2/r - mg = (1)(25)/1 - (1)(10) = 25 - 10 = 15\ \text{N}$. The trap is $25\ \text{N}$, the full centripetal force $mv^2/r$, which forgets that gravity already provides part of it and so overcounts the tension.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A horizontal force of $30\ \text{N}$ is applied to a $5\ \text{kg}$ block on a rough floor, and the block moves at constant velocity. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the coefficient of kinetic friction?
questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero net force, so friction balances the applied force: $f_k = 30\ \text{N}$. Since $f_k = \mu_k N = \mu_k mg$, we get $\mu_k = 30/(5 \times 10) = 30/50 = 0.6$. The trap is $0.3$, which divides the applied force by twice the weight, or equivalently uses $N = 2mg$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ object experiences two horizontal forces: $8\ \text{N}$ due east and $6\ \text{N}$ due north. What is the magnitude of its acceleration?
questions.explanation: The two forces are perpendicular, so the net force has magnitude $\sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10\ \text{N}$. The acceleration is then $a = F_{net}/m = 10/2 = 5\ \text{m/s}^2$. The trap is $7\ \text{m/s}^2$, which adds the forces as $8 + 6 = 14\ \text{N}$ and divides by $2$, ignoring that perpendicular forces combine by Pythagoras, not by simple addition.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A skydiver of mass $80\ \text{kg}$ falls and reaches terminal velocity. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the air-resistance force on the skydiver at terminal velocity?
questions.explanation: At terminal velocity the speed is constant, so the acceleration and net force are zero. The upward air resistance must then exactly balance the downward weight: $F_{air} = mg = 80(10) = 800\ \text{N}$. The trap is $0\ \text{N}$, which confuses zero net force with zero air resistance; the air-resistance force is large, it simply cancels the weight.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A block is pressed against a vertical wall by a horizontal force and stays at rest. Which force balances the block's weight?
options: The normal force from the wall, acting horizontally.
The weight has no balancing force, so the block accelerates.
The friction force from the wall, acting upward.
questions.explanation: The weight acts straight down, and the only other vertical force available is the friction between the block and the wall, which must act upward to hold the block in place. The normal force and the applied push are both horizontal and cannot balance a vertical weight. The trap is the applied horizontal force: it presses the block to the wall and sets the maximum friction, but being horizontal it does nothing to balance the downward weight directly.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block on a horizontal frictionless surface is pulled by a string with a tension that increases the speed from rest to $8\ \text{m/s}$ in $4\ \text{s}$. What is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation: The acceleration is $a = \Delta v/\Delta t = 8/4 = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$. On a frictionless surface the tension is the only horizontal force, so $T = ma = 2(2) = 4\ \text{N}$. The trap is $16\ \text{N}$, which multiplies the mass by the final speed $8\ \text{m/s}$, confusing momentum-per-unit-something with force.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $3\ \text{kg}$ block on a frictionless horizontal table is connected over a light frictionless pulley to a $2\ \text{kg}$ block hanging freely. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the acceleration of the system and the string tension.
questions.solution: The driving force is the hanging weight $m_2 g = 2 \times 10 = 20\ \text{N}$, and the total mass set in motion is $m_1 + m_2 = 5\ \text{kg}$, so the acceleration is $a = $ {{1}} metres per second squared. The tension is the only horizontal force on the table block, $T = m_1 a = 3 \times a = $ {{2}} newtons.
questions.explanation: The acceleration is $20/5 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$, and the tension is $3 \times 4 = 12\ \text{N}$. Checking on the hanging block, $m_2 g - T = m_2 a$ gives $20 - 12 = 8 = 2 \times 4$, consistent.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $2\ \text{kg}$ block slides down a plane inclined at $37^\circ$ ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$) with coefficient of kinetic friction $0.5$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the along-plane acceleration and the normal force.
questions.solution: The gravity component down the plane gives $g\sin\theta = 10 \times 0.6 = 6\ \text{m/s}^2$ and friction opposes with $\mu_k g\cos\theta = 0.5 \times 10 \times 0.8 = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$, so the acceleration is $a = $ {{1}} metres per second squared. The normal force is $N = mg\cos\theta = 2 \times 10 \times 0.8 = $ {{2}} newtons.
questions.explanation: The net acceleration is $6 - 4 = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$, and the normal force is $2(10)(0.8) = 16\ \text{N}$. The normal force uses the cosine of the incline angle, the along-plane gravity uses the sine.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $4\ \text{kg}$ block rests on a rough floor with coefficient of static friction $0.5$ and coefficient of kinetic friction $0.3$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. A horizontal force of $15\ \text{N}$ is applied.
questions.solution: The maximum static friction is $\mu_s mg = 0.5 \times 40 = $ {{1}} newtons. Since the applied $15\ \text{N}$ is below this limit, the block stays at rest and the actual friction force equals {{2}} newtons. In this situation the block {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The maximum static friction is $20\ \text{N}$, which exceeds the applied $15\ \text{N}$, so static friction matches the push at $15\ \text{N}$ and the block does not move. Kinetic friction $\mu_k mg = 12\ \text{N}$ never comes into play because sliding never starts.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A person of mass $50\ \text{kg}$ stands on a scale in a lift. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the scale reading when the lift accelerates upward at $3\ \text{m/s}^2$, and when it accelerates downward at $3\ \text{m/s}^2$.
questions.solution: Upward acceleration gives $N = m(g + a) = 50 \times 13 = $ {{1}} newtons. Downward acceleration gives $N = m(g - a) = 50 \times 7 = $ {{2}} newtons. The person feels heavier when the lift accelerates {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Upward, $N = 50(13) = 650\ \text{N}$; downward, $N = 50(7) = 350\ \text{N}$. The apparent weight rises above $mg = 500\ \text{N}$ for upward acceleration and falls below it for downward acceleration.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: An Atwood machine has masses $1\ \text{kg}$ and $4\ \text{kg}$ on a light string over a frictionless pulley. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the acceleration of the masses and the string tension.
questions.solution: The net driving force is $(m_2 - m_1)g = (4 - 1) \times 10 = 30\ \text{N}$ over a total mass of $5\ \text{kg}$, so the acceleration is $a = $ {{1}} metres per second squared. Applying $T - m_1 g = m_1 a$ to the lighter mass, the tension is $T = m_1(g + a) = 1 \times (10 + a) = $ {{2}} newtons.
questions.explanation: The acceleration is $30/5 = 6\ \text{m/s}^2$, and the tension is $1(10 + 6) = 16\ \text{N}$. Checking on the heavier mass, $m_2 g - T = 40 - 16 = 24 = 4 \times 6$, consistent.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A block on a rough plane is slowly tilted up. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.75$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find the angle of repose (the angle at which the block just begins to slip), and the relation it satisfies.
questions.solution: At the point of slipping, the along-plane weight equals the maximum static friction, which after cancelling $mg$ gives $\tan\theta = \mu_s$. With $\mu_s = 0.75$, the angle is $\theta = \arctan(0.75) \approx $ {{1}} degrees. The quantity that equals $\mu_s$ at the critical angle is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Balancing $mg\sin\theta = \mu_s mg\cos\theta$ gives $\tan\theta = \mu_s = 0.75$, so $\theta = \arctan(0.75) \approx 36.9^\circ$, about $37^\circ$. The angle of repose depends only on $\mu_s$, not on the mass of the block.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics
